title: Tempered dark chocolate
subtitle: Tabling-method temper for couverture
excerpt: Tempered dark chocolate by the classical tabling method: melt at 50°C, work two-thirds on a cold surface down to 28–29°C, return to the bowl to raise the temperature to 31–32°C. Use for moulding, dipping, and decorations. Five hundred grams.
sourceNotes: Auguste Escoffier and earlier nineteenth-century French confectioners document the tabling method (le tablage) for tempering couverture. Pellaprat, L'Art Culinaire Moderne (pre-1928, public domain in French): documents the temperature windows for dark, milk and white chocolate.

recipe.freezeNotes: Tempered chocolate keeps at cool room temperature for months in a sealed tin.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Re-temper any leftover chocolate by melting and tabling again.

recipeTools.notes: A digital probe; sugar thermometers do not read at this range.
Or a metal palette knife — for the tabling.
Metal — to gather the chocolate on the marble.
Marble or other cold stone surface.

glossaryTerms.definition: The temperature ranges that produce stable Form V cocoa-butter crystals in chocolate. Dark chocolate: melt at 50°C, cool to 28–29°C, work back up to 31–32°C. Milk chocolate: melt at 45°C, cool to 27–28°C, work back to 29–30°C. White chocolate: melt at 45°C, cool to 26–27°C, work back to 28–29°C. Correctly tempered chocolate sets snappy and glossy and resists bloom; mistempered chocolate looks dull, sets slowly, and develops white streaks on the surface.

content.text: Tempering chocolate is the process of melting it and then cooling it through a specific temperature curve so the cocoa butter sets in the stable Form V crystal structure. The result is chocolate that snaps cleanly, shines glossy, sets at room temperature, and resists the white bloom that mistempered chocolate develops over time. The tabling method works two-thirds of the melted chocolate on a cold marble surface to seed the Form V crystals, then returns the cooled chocolate to the remaining warm chocolate to raise the temperature back into the working window.

Use couverture chocolate (at least 30% cocoa butter) not eating chocolate; the cocoa butter content controls the temper. A digital probe thermometer is essential — the 1°C between in-temper and out-of-temper matters.

items.prepNote: couverture, 70% cocoa, finely chopped or callets

content.text: Set a wide saucepan with 3 cm of water on low heat. Set a metal bowl over the pan, base just above the water. Add the chopped {{dark-chocolate}} and stir gently with a spatula as it melts. Check the temperature with the probe. Stop when the chocolate reaches 50°C and is fully melted; lift the bowl from the heat.
Table two-thirds, down to 28–29°C — see the

Pour two-thirds of the melted chocolate onto a cool marble surface. Use a palette knife and metal scraper to work the chocolate continuously — spread it thin, scrape it back together, push it out again. Keep working for 4–5 minutes; the chocolate will thicken visibly as it cools. Check the temperature; stop at 28–29°C. The chocolate should hold a peak briefly when lifted on the palette knife.

Scrape the cooled chocolate back into the bowl with the warm reserved third. Stir gently to combine; the temperature should settle around 31–32°C. If too cool, set the bowl over the warm water for 5–10 seconds at a time, stirring, checking the temperature. Test the temper: dip the tip of a spatula and set on a piece of baking paper. It should set hard and glossy in 3 minutes at cool room temperature, with a clean snap when broken. If it stays soft or sets dull, the temper failed — re-melt and re-table.
